trials.raw_response: The `-r` flag in the `cp` command is a short form for `--recursive`. It tells `cp` to descend into subdirectories (and their contents) rather than stopping when it reaches a directory boundary. For example, `cp -r src/ dst/` copies everything in `src/` into `dst/`, creating the necessary intermediate directories along the way.

The `-r` flag in the `cp` command is a short form for `--recursive`. It tells `cp` to descend into subdirectories (and their contents) rather than stopping when it reaches a directory boundary. For example, `cp -r src/ dst/` copies everything in `src/` into `dst/`, creating the necessary intermediate directories along the way.

The `-r` flag in the `cp` command is a short form for `--recursive`. It tells `cp` to descend into subdirectories (and their contents) rather than stopping when it reaches a directory boundary. For example, `cp -r src/ dst/` copies everything in `src/` into `dst/`, creating the necessary intermediate directories along the way.

A pipe connects the stdout (standard output) of one command directly to the stdin (standard input) of another. This lets commands run sequentially without needing to write anything to a file or terminal. For example, `cat file.txt | sort` shows the sorted content of a file directly on the terminal.

A pipe connects the stdout (standard output) of one command directly to the stdin (standard input) of another. This lets commands run sequentially without needing to write anything to a file or terminal. For example, `cat file.txt | sort` shows the sorted content of a file directly on the terminal.
